question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a smart contract simulation (eth_call/eth_estimateGas) that internally calls getWritableStates() for TokenService, obtains a WritableSingletonState for STAKING_NETWORK_REWARDS_STATE_ID, and sets an arbitrarily low staking reward value so that subsequent fee calculations for other users reflect manipulated reward parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that writableStates is a ConcurrentHashMap that is never cleared (only readableStates is removed on commit via the onCommit lambda) to cause a previously modified fee-related WritableKVState to persist across multiple transaction simulations, leading to incorrect fee computation for all subsequent callers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user submit a crafted eth_call that causes getWritableStates() to cache a MapWritableStates instance for a fee-related service (e.g., TokenService), and because writableStates.computeIfAbsent never re-creates the entry, all future calls to getWritableStates() for that service return the same stale/modified writable state, permanently skewing fee calculations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user trigger a simulation path that calls getWritableStates() for a service not present in the states map, causing EmptyWritableStates to be cached in writableStates, so that any legitimate internal fee-service write attempt subsequently throws IllegalArgumentException, effectively denying fee state updates?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user race two concurrent eth_call requests against getWritableStates() such that the underlying states HashMap (not ConcurrentHashMap) is read concurrently, causing a partially initialized fee-related service state map to be wrapped in a MapWritableStates and cached, resulting in missing fee state entries that cause zero-fee or incorrect-fee outcomes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getWritableStates() calls getReadableStates(serviceName).get(stateId) to build the writable KV state, and if getReadableStates() returns a cached empty MapReadableStates (due to a prior cache miss), the resulting MapWritableKVState has no backing data, causing fee schedule lookups to return null and fees to be computed as zero?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getReadableStates()] Can an unprivileged user trigger a cache miss in getReadableStates() for a fee-related service by racing a commit() call (which invokes readableStates.remove(serviceName)) with a concurrent getReadableStates() call, causing a new MapReadableStates to be built from a stale or partially updated states map, leading to incorrect fee schedule data being served?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getReadableStates()] Can an unprivileged user submit a high volume of concurrent eth_call requests to force repeated computeIfAbsent executions on the readableStates ConcurrentHashMap for the same fee-related service, causing multiple MapReadableStates instances to be constructed from the non-thread-safe states HashMap simultaneously, resulting in a data race that corrupts the fee state cache?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initSingletonStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that initSingletonStates() registers DefaultSingleton instances for STAKING_NETWORK_REWARDS_STATE_ID and NODE_REWARDS_STATE_ID into the non-thread-safe states HashMap, and if a concurrent getReadableStates() or getWritableStates() call reads states during initialization, it may observe an incomplete fee-related singleton registration, causing fee calculations to use null or default reward values?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initSingletonStates()] Can an unprivileged user cause a DefaultSingleton for NODE_REWARDS_STATE_ID (backed by AtomicReference) to be set to an attacker-controlled value through a simulation that internally calls FunctionWritableSingletonState.put(), which buffers the value in WritableSingletonStateBase, and if commit() is called, putIntoDataSource() is a no-op but the buffered value is returned by get() for the duration of the simulation, causing fee calculations within that simulation to use attacker-supplied node reward parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initSingletonStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that DefaultSingleton extends AtomicReference<Object> and is shared across all callers of getReadableStates() and getWritableStates(), so that a simulation that modifies the DefaultSingleton's AtomicReference value (via a code path that bypasses FunctionWritableSingletonState's no-op putIntoDataSource) permanently alters the STAKING_NETWORK_REWARDS_STATE_ID value for all subsequent fee calculations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initKVStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that EVM_HOOK_STORAGE_STATE_ID and EVM_HOOK_STATES_STATE_ID are initialized with empty MapReadableKVState instances backed by plain HashMaps, and by triggering a simulation that writes fee-related contract storage into the writable EVM hook state, cause the fee calculation logic to read attacker-injected values from the hook storage, modifying effective transaction fees?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initKVStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that SCHEDULED_COUNTS_STATE_ID and SCHEDULED_USAGES_STATE_ID are initialized with empty MapReadableKVState instances, and by injecting crafted scheduled transaction count data through a writable state obtained via getWritableStates(), cause the fee multiplier or congestion-based fee calculation to be skewed outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initQueueStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that initQueueStates() creates a single shared LinkedList for TRANSACTION_RECEIPTS_STATE_ID, and ListWritableQueueState.addToDataSource() directly adds to this backing LinkedList, so that a simulation that adds crafted transaction receipts to the queue causes the receipt cache to reflect attacker-controlled transaction outcomes, potentially affecting fee-related receipt lookups?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: initQueueStates()] Can an unprivileged user flood the TRANSACTION_RECEIPTS_STATE_ID LinkedList by triggering many simulations that each add entries via ListWritableQueueState, causing unbounded memory growth and eventual OOM that disrupts fee calculation services for all users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user craft a simulation that obtains a WritableKVState for SCHEDULE_ID_BY_EQUALITY_STATE_ID via getWritableStates(ScheduleService.NAME), injects a crafted schedule entry with a manipulated fee, and because MapWritableKVState.putIntoDataSource() calls put() on the write cache, causes subsequent fee lookups for scheduled transactions to return the attacker-injected fee value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the absence of any authorization check in getWritableStates() to obtain a writable view of any service's fee-related state (e.g., TokenService fee schedule) and modify it within a simulation, causing the simulation to compute and return a fee estimate that is orders of magnitude lower than the actual network fee, misleading users into underpaying?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getWritableStates() wraps each ReadableKVState in a MapWritableKVState without any value validation, allowing the user to put() a fee-related KV entry with a negative or zero amount, causing the fee calculation engine to compute a negative fee or zero fee for a transaction type?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the onCommit lambda in MapWritableStates only calls readableStates.remove(serviceName) but does NOT remove the entry from writableStates, so that after a commit, the same MapWritableStates (with its in-memory write cache) is reused for the next simulation, causing fee modifications from one simulation to bleed into the next?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user trigger a simulation that calls getWritableStates() for ContractService, obtains a MapWritableKVState for EVM_HOOK_STORAGE_STATE_ID, and injects a crafted storage slot value that the EVM fee hook reads to override the gas price or fee multiplier, causing the simulation to report a manipulated fee?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getWritableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getWritableStates() for an unknown service name returns EmptyWritableStates and caches it, so that if a fee-related service is later registered (e.g., via a hot-reload or dynamic registration), the cached EmptyWritableStates prevents the new service's fee state from being accessible, causing fee calculations to fail or return defaults?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getReadableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getReadableStates() caches a MapReadableStates built from the states map at the time of first access, and if the states map is later updated (e.g., a new fee-related KV state is added), the cached MapReadableStates does not reflect the update, causing fee lookups to use stale fee schedule data?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getReadableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getReadableStates() returns a new empty MapReadableStates for unknown service names and caches it, so that a typo or case-sensitivity difference in a service name used by the fee calculation engine causes it to always receive an empty state, computing fees as if no fee schedule exists?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getReadableStates()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that getReadableStates() wraps Queue instances in ListReadableQueueState using the shared backing LinkedList, so that a concurrent modification of the TRANSACTION_RECEIPTS_STATE_ID queue (via a writable state) while getReadableStates() is iterating over it causes a ConcurrentModificationException that disrupts fee-related receipt processing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/state/MirrorNodeState.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: setHash()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that setHash() is a no-op to submit a simulation with a crafted state hash that would normally be rejected by integrity checks, allowing the simulation to proceed with a manipulated fee state that would otherwise be detected as tampered?
